title-group.article-title: Potential Effects of Sacubitril/Valsartan on the Cardio-ankle Vascular Index in Hypertensive Patients

contrib-group.aff: 1Division of Cardiovascular Medicine, Department of Internal Medicine, Toho University Sakura Medical Center, Japan
2Department of Clinical Functional Physiology, Toho University Sakura Medical Center, Japan

fn.p: Correspondence to Dr. Kazuhiro Shimizu, k432@sakura.med.toho-u.ac.jp

permissions.copyright-statement: Copyright © 2025 by The Japanese Society of Internal Medicine

license.license-p: The Internal Medicine is an Open Access journal distributed under the Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial-NoDerivatives 4.0 International License. To view the details of this license, please visit (https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-nd/4.0/).

sec.title: Objective
sec.p: Angiotensin receptor-neprilysin inhibitors (ARNIs) have been widely used as a treatment for heart failure. However, they have only been approved for the treatment of hypertension in a limited number of countries. We investigated the effect of ARNIs on arterial stiffness using the cardio-ankle vascular index (CAVI) in patients with hypertension to reveal the mechanism underlying cardiovascular interaction.

sec.title: Methods
sec.p: We investigated the utility of ARNIs in patients in whom arterial stiffness was assessed using the CAVI before and after ARNI administration.

sec.title: Patients
sec.p: In this retrospective observational study, we enrolled 50 patients with hypertension treated with ARNIs between January 2021 and 2023. Forty-eight of the 50 patients (96%) were switched from angiotensin II receptor blockers due to inadequate antihypertensive control.

sec.title: Results
sec.p: The systolic blood pressure (BP), diastolic BP, and CAVI were significantly decreased by ARNI administration [systolic BP: 145 (135, 162) to 131 (123, 143) mmHg (p=0.000); diastolic BP: 92 (78, 100) to 82 (74, 89) mmHg (p=0.000); and CAVI: 9.9 (9.1, 10.5) to 9.5 (8.8, 10.2) (p=0.005), respectively].

sec.title: Conclusion
sec.p: Hypertension treatment with ARNIs may improve not only the blood pressure but also the CAVI, reducing the afterload on the heart.

sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: There have been several reports of sacubitril/valsartan, colloquially known as angiotensin receptor-neprilysin inhibitors (ARNIs), improving cardiac remodeling in heart failure with reduced ejection fraction (HFrEF). Therefore, ARNIs are now used worldwide as basic drugs for the treatment of HFrEF (1,2). Angiotensin II receptor blockers (ARBs) inhibit angiotensin II receptor type 1 and primarily block arteriolar contraction and sodium retention effects of the renin-angiotensin system. ARNIs are dual-acting angiotensin II receptor and neprilysin inhibitors. The usual adult dosage for hypertension is 200 mg ARNI once daily, while the maximum dosage is 400 mg/day. Each 200 mg tablet of ARNI contains 97.2 mg of sacubitril and 102.8 mg of valsartan.

ARBs have already been shown to have an excellent ameliorating effect on left ventricular hypertrophy in the losartan intervention for endpoint reduction in hypertension (LIFE) trial (3). Left ventricular remodeling caused by hypertrophy is known to be a risk factor in cardiovascular diseases (4,5). In fact, ARNIs have been reported to have superior antihypertensive efficacy and to significantly improve the left ventricular mass index (LVMI) compared with olmesartan in clinical trials (6,7). However, few countries have approved ARNIs for the treatment of hypertension. Furthermore, the mechanisms underlying cardiac remodeling improvement are not fully understood.

In 2006 the cardio-ankle vascular index (CAVI) was developed as an indicator of arterial stiffness from the origin of the aorta to the ankle (8). The CAVI is a numerical index of arterial stiffness derived from the stiffness parameter beta theory and the Bramwell-Hill equation for pulse wave velocity (PWV). The most important feature of the CAVI is its independence from blood pressure at the time of measurement (9,10). Because of this feature, the CAVI is highly useful for assessing vasoactive substances, in contrast to the PWV, which fluctuates in parallel with blood pressure at the time of measurement.

Recently, we reported the case of a hypertensive patient with a high CAVI treated with an ARNI (11). In this case, the CAVI decreased with increasing ARNI dose. Furthermore, the CAVI subsequently increased with decreasing ARNI dose, regardless of the blood pressure value at the time of measurement. Hypertension is one of the primary causes of heart failure. We believe that the phenomenon observed in this patient may explain the ameliorative effect of ARNIs on heart failure from the viewpoint of cardiovascular interaction. Therefore, we retrospectively investigated changes in the CAVI in hypertensive patients receiving ARNIs at our institution.

sec.title: Material and Methods
sec.p: We enrolled hypertensive patients who visited the Department of Cardiology Division of Toho University Sakura Medical Center (Japan), and were treated with ARNIs between 2021 and 2023. The inclusion criteria were outpatients with hypertension whose CAVI was measured before and after ARNI treatment and whose medical records confirmed no change in other concomitant medications at least three months prior to receiving ARNI treatment. The exclusion criteria were as follows: symptomatic heart failure, acute myocardial infarction, old myocardial infarction, cardiomyopathy, open-heart surgery, non-sinus rhythm, atherosclerosis obliterans (ankle-brachial index ＜0.9), pulmonary hypertensive disease, and moderate or severe valvular disease. We analyzed 50 consecutive patients who underwent CAVI measurements before and after ARNI administration.

Hypertension was defined as systolic blood pressure (SBP) ≥140 mmHg and/or diastolic blood pressure (DBP) ≥90 mmHg or a prescription for antihypertensive agents. Diabetes mellitus was defined as glycated hemoglobin ≥6.5% or a prescription of antidiabetic treatment. Dyslipidemia was defined as a low-density lipoprotein cholesterol concentration ≥140 mg/dL, high-density lipoprotein cholesterol concentration ＜40 mg/dL, triglyceride concentration ≥150 mg/dL, or requiring antihyperlipidemic treatment. Chronic kidney disease is determined by an estimated glomerular filtration rate of ＜60 mL/min/1.73 m2 and/or proteinuria.

sec.title: Measurement of the CAVI
sec.p: The CAVI was measured as a physiological function test, as part of routine medical care, by expert staff in the clinical functional physiology department of our hospital. CAVI values were determined from measurements obtained with a vascular screening system (VaSera3000; Fukuda Denshi, Tokyo, Japan) using a previously described method (8,9). The CAVI value was determined with the following equation, derived from the Bramwell-Hill equation:
CAVI=a{(2p/ΔP)×In (Ps/Pd) PWV2}+b

where Ps and Pd are the systolic and diastolic blood pressures, respectively; PWV is the pulse-wave velocity from the origin of the aorta to the tibial artery-femoral artery junction; ΔP is the difference between the systolic and diastolic blood pressure (i.e. Ps - Pd); p is the blood density; and a and b are constants. The CAVI value was automatically analyzed using the VaSera system. We used SBP and DBP values determined from measurements on the right side in this study.

sec.title: Statistical analyses
sec.p: Normally distributed data were assessed using the Shapiro-Wilk test. Categorical variables were presented as numbers (percentages). Continuous variables were expressed as mean±standard deviation or median (interquartile range), as appropriate. Fisher's exact test was used to analyze categorical data. Comparisons before and after ARNI administration were made using a paired t-test or Wilcoxon's signed-rank test, as appropriate. All patients and the ultrasound-evaluated group were compared using the Mann-Whitney U test.
The SPSS software package (PASW Statistics 25, Chicago, USA) was used for all statistical analyses. Statistical significance was set at p＜0.05.

sec.title: Patient profile
sec.p: Fifty consecutive patients with CAVI measured before and after ARNI administration at our outpatient clinic were retrospectively investigated. The interval between CAVI measurements was approximately six months. Patient characteristics are shown in Table 1. ARNIs are not the first-line treatment for hypertension in Japan. Calcium channel blockers and ARBs are commonly used as first-line agents for hypertension treatment. In this study, 48 of 50 patients (96%) switched from ARBs because of inadequate blood pressure control. Automatic home blood pressure monitoring is widely used in Japan. Blood pressure was measured at home in the morning and evening. Patients record their daily blood pressure values in a notebook or on a smartphone, and outpatient physicians often use these records to provide care for hypertension. The ARBs used prior to use were as follows: azilsartan 20 mg in 1 patient, azilsartan 40 mg in 3 patients, irbesartan 100 mg in 2 patients, olmesartan 20 mg in 5 patients, olmesartan 40 mg in 1 patient, candesartan 8 mg in 2 patients, telmisartan 40 mg in 4 patients, valsartan 80 mg in 24 patients, valsartan (80 mg)/hydrochlorothiazide (6.25 mg) combination tablet in 1 patient, and losartan potassium (100 mg)/hydrochlorothiazide (12.5 mg) combination tablet in 5 patients. The breakdown of ARNI doses was 100 mg in four cases, 200 mg in 29 cases, and 400 mg in 17 cases.

fn.p: BMI: body mass index, BP: blood pressure, CAVI: cardio ankle vascular index, BNP: brain natriuretic peptide, DM: diabetes mellitus, DL: dyslipidemia, CaB: calcium blocker, ARB: angiotensin II receptor blocker, MRA: mineralocorticoid receptor antagonist, Beta B: beta blocker

sec.p: As shown in Fig. 1, the SBP, DBP, and CAVI were significantly decreased by the administration of ARNI. Serum creatine levels did not change markedly, increasing from 0.86 (0.73, 1.05) to 0.90 (0.76, 1.05) (p=0.303), and serum brain natriuretic peptide (BNP) level slightly increased from 32.2 (15.6, 56.0) to 33.5 (17.3, 94.5) (p=0.018).

caption.p: The changes in the blood pressure and CAVI after ARNI administration. BP: blood pressure, CAVI: cardio-ankle vascular index, ARNI: angiotensin receptor-neprilysin inhibitor

sec.title: Changes in patients who had received valsartan 80 mg
sec.p: Twenty-five patients were initially treated with valsartan 80 mg but were switched to an ARNI due to inadequate control as per the guidelines. The breakdown of ARNI use across patients was as follows: 100 mg/day for 1 (4%), 200 mg/day for 16 (64%), and 400 mg/day for 8 (32%). A 200 mg ARNI tablet contains 97.2 mg of sacubitril and 102.8 mg of valsartan. Therefore, we studied parameter changes in patients who switched from 80 mg valsartan to 200 mg ARNI. As in the overall analysis, the CAVI showed a significant improvement (Table 2).

caption.p: Parameter Changes in Patients Switched from Valsartan 80 mg to ARNI 200 mg (n=16).

fn.p: BP: blood pressure, HR: heart rate, CAVI: cardio-ankle vascular index, BNP: brain natriuretic peptide

sec.title: Changes in echocardiographic parameters
sec.p: In 21 of the 50 patients, echocardiography was performed on the same day as the CAVI measurement before and after ARNI administration. As shown in Table 1, there were no statistically significant differences in the patient backgrounds. An echocardiographic assessment was later performed using the speckle-tracking method, in addition to the usual analysis. Left ventricular global longitudinal strain (LVGLS) and atrial strain were quantified offline using the EchoPAC PC system version 113 (General Electric Healthcare, Chicago, USA). The LVGLS was calculated as the average negative peak of the longitudinal strain in the four-chamber, two-chamber, and apical long-axis directions. Left atrial (LA) phasic functions were evaluated as reservoir, conduit, and pump functions. The LA strain was determined as the average of the values obtained for the six LA segments in the apical four-chamber and two-chamber views. As shown in Table 3, the LVMI, LA reservoir strain, and LA pump strain were significantly improved.

fn.p: Data are presented as mean±standard deviation or median (interquartile range) as appropriate.

LVMI: left ventricular mass index, RWT: relative wall thickness, LVEF: left ventricular ejection fraction, E: peak early diastolic velocity of transmitral flow, e’: peak early diastolic mitral annular motion velocity, LVGLS: left ventricular global longitudinal strain, LAVI: left atrial volume index

sec.title: Discussion
sec.p: Left ventricular (LV) hypertrophy is often induced by hypertension, and appropriate hypertension treatment is important to prevent future cardiovascular diseases. Olmesartan is an antihypertensive agent for ARBs. Previous studies have already reported that hypertensive patients treated with ARNI displayed greater reductions in LV mass than those treated with olmesartan after 12 and 52 weeks of treatment (7). Indeed, we were able to confirm an improvement in the LVMI before and after ARNI treatment on an ultrasound assessment, similar to previous magnetic resonance imaging reports. Moreover, the LA reservoir and pump strains improved. These results indicate that the treatment of hypertension with ARNI improves the afterload of the heart. Despite this evidence, only a few countries have obtained insurance indications for their use in the treatment of hypertension.

The novelty of the present observational study is that we used the CAVI technique, which is unaffected by blood pressure at the time of measurement. The CAVI has been established as an index of atherosclerosis progression (8,9) and reflects functional stiffness, which is affected by arterial smooth muscle cell contractions. We have conducted experiments on acute-phase responses to the CAVI using alpha-blockers, beta-blockers, and nitroglycerin in humans and rabbits (10,12,13). Values vary from minutes to hours and return to baseline when the drug effect wears off. Given the observation timeframe spanning months in the present study, we considered that arterial constriction following ARNI administration represents a chronic-phase response.

Improvements in the CAVI are, in other words, improvements in cardiac afterload. Furthermore, the serum BNP level was slightly increased in our observational study. We suspected that this phenomenon might be due to an initial change capturing the effect of sacubitril. BNP itself has been shown to have a relaxation effect on vascular smooth muscles, causing arterial dilation and leading to reduced blood pressure and ventricular afterload in the heart (14-17). In this observational study, all patients had been stable outpatients with hypertension for at least three months prior to receiving ARNIs. As there were no changes in medication other than ARNIs during the observation period, we considered the influence of other drugs on the results of this study to be infinitesimally small. We speculated that the relaxation of vascular smooth muscle cells may be the main reason why ARNIs, which are widely used for heart failure, are expected to be useful for treating hypertension.

As shown in Fig. 2, we considered the cardiovascular interaction scheme of ARNI. Miyoshi reported in a large cohort prospective five-year observational study (CAVI-J) that an increased CAVI was associated with the development of cardiovascular events (18). There is thus a need for clinical observational studies with the potential to improve the CAVI (19).

caption.p: Effects of ARNIs on the heart and blood vessels.

sec.p: Our observational study showed the possibility that ARNIs can reduce arterial constriction through improving the CAVI. Left ventricular hypertrophy associated with long-term hypertension is a risk factor for the future occurrence of heart failure. ARNI are not currently the first-line drug for hypertension treatment. However, based on the present study, ARNI may be an option for treatment-resistant hypertensive patients with high CAVI, potentially contributing to future healthcare cost reduction.

sec.title: Study limitations
sec.p: To our knowledge, this report is the first to investigate the changes in the CAVI among hypertensive patients treated with an ARNI. However, this study had some limitations. First, it had a single-center retrospective design and a relatively small sample size. Second, we did not include a control group in this study. Third, the types of antihypertensive medication administered prior to ARNI treatment and subsequent ARNI use were not the same. Fourth, sacubitril is converted to its active form in the body and inhibits neprilysin. The clinical significance of this slight increase in the BNP level is unclear. Another limitation of this observational study is that we only had data on BNP, as BNP testing is used in our hospital, while NT-proBNP is an outsourced test. Further prospective studies are needed to confirm these results.
However, we believe that this study genuinely assessed the effects of ARNIs in the real world, as examinations were conducted before and after ARNI administration, without other modifications.

sec.title: Conclusion
sec.p: Hypertensive patients with a high CAVI may require increased attention for future cardiovascular events. The results of this study suggest that the treatment of hypertension with ARNIs may improve not only the blood pressure but also the CAVI as afterload of the heart.

The study was conducted in accordance with the Declaration of Helsinki and was approved by the Institutional Review Board of the Ethics Committee of Toho University Sakura Medical Center (S23011). Informed consent was obtained in the form of opt-out on the website.
The authors state that they have no Conflict of Interest (COI).

ack.p: The authors thank the staff of the Clinical Functional Physiology Department, Toho University Sakura Medical Center, for their help in conducting this study. The authors thank honorary Prof. Kohji Shirai for useful comments and discussions.
